macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf and Previous Discussion on Oaths Imposed by Husbands

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of previous daf
The husband's ability to exempt his wife from oaths regarding fund management
Distinction between neder and shevu'ah and their applications

macro_segments.title: The Amoraim Debate: Two Interpretations of the Mishnah's Opening

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's position: the exemption refers to apotropaya (business management)
Rav Nachman's position: the exemption refers to pogemet ketubah (partial payment)
Rav Mordechai presents the debate before Rav Ashi

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Resolution: The Two Positions Address Different Parts of the Mishnah

micro_segments.title: Why the apotropaya scenario is unusual and why Rav Ashi rejects it as the primary meaning
Rav Nachman's view is the plain sense of the Mishnah's opening
Rav Yehuda's statement applies to the Mishnah's ending, not the beginning

macro_segments.title: The Case of Death and Burial: When Can Heirs Impose an Oath Between Mita and Kevura?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's distinction between when the manager is exempt and when heirs can impose oaths
Rav Yehuda: apotropaya business dealings while husband was alive are always exempt
Rav Yehuda's additional point: between death and burial the heirs can impose an oath
Rav Matna's counter-position: even between death and burial, no oath is imposed
The Nahardai teaching on urgent sales without announcement and its relevance
Rashi's explanation: the woman should not be burdened with an oath for hasty decisions made under pressure
Tosafot's alternative: fear that she will abandon her post if threatened with an oath

macro_segments.title: The Language of Exemption and Its Scope: Rabba and Rav Yosef's Interpretations

micro_segments.title: Distinction between 'without a neder' and 'clean from a neder' regarding binding power on heirs
Rabba's view: only strong language ('clean from oath') exempts heirs
Rav Yosef's view: 'clean' language does not exempt the manager from any oath
Rav Zakai's ruling: language distinctions do not matter; the critical distinction is possessive language

macro_segments.title: Abba Shaul's Blanket Protection and Its Practical Limits

micro_segments.title: Abba Shaul's position: no language variant exempts the woman from any oath, neither to husband nor to heirs
The caveat: she must still swear when collecting from heirs as a creditor on the ketubah
The distinction between oath obligated to the husband versus oath as a general creditor

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Pogemet Ketubah: When a Woman Admits Partial Payment

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's cases of pogemet: partial admission and single witness testimony
Cases involving meshubadim (liened property) and non-present debtors
Detailed enumeration of scenarios in the Mishnah

macro_segments.title: Is Pogemet Ketubah a Biblical or Rabbinic Oath?

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama's theory that pogemet is a standard biblical modeh bemiktzat case
Rava's first refutation: in a true modeh bemiktzat, the debtor swears and is exempt; here the woman swears and collects
Rava's second refutation: no biblical oath is administered regarding land (ketubah involves a lien on property)
Rava's conclusion: this is a rabbinic oath designed to encourage careful recollection by the woman
The psychology: the payer is more attentive than the payee to details of payment

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Cases Clarified: Witnesses, Ledgers, and the Exact Amount

micro_segments.title: First question (ibaya lehu): if there are witnesses to the first payment, must she still swear?
Citation of the mishna that oaths sworn in Torah exempt the swearer from payment; pogemet is rabbinic and collects via oath
Second question: if she concedes in precise penny-amounts, can we say she's truthful and doesn't need to swear?
The dilemma: precision suggests honesty, or it could be a clever ruse to avoid the oath
Third question: if she concedes the ketubah amount itself was less (not that part has been paid), must she swear?
The resolution: if she concedes only to a lesser original amount, not partial payment, she collects without oath
How can she collect on a false ketubah document? Via the trust agreement between them

topical_tags.term: pogemet ketubah (woman admits partial payment of ketubah)
Rav Yehuda amar Rav (Rav Yehuda in the name of Rav)
Rav Nachman amar Rabba Bar Avuha (Rav Nachman in the name of Rabba Bar Avuha)
miga (argumentation: 'if you could say nothing, you could say this')
modeh bemiktzat (one who concedes part of a claim)
between mita and kevura (between death and burial)
she'avar (the past; which past is included)
